identity.canonical_title: Geometry: Curvature of Spheres and Tori: K_inner

theory.canonical_statement: trast, a torus (a doughnut-shaped surface, topologically $S^1 \times S^1$) exhibits varying curvature – positive on the outer rim and negative on the inner rim. Parameterizing the torus with major radius $R$ (from center of hole to center of tube) and minor radius $r$ (tube radius), the Gaussian curvature at a point is \begin{equation} K_{\text{torus}}(\phi) ;=; \frac{\cos\phi}{,r, (R + r\cos\phi),},, \end{equation} where $\phi$ is the angle around the tube cross-section. At the outer equator ($\phi=0$) one finds $K_{\text{outer}} = \frac{1}{R, (R+r)}$, while at the inner equator ($\phi=\pi$) $K_{\text{inner}} = -,\frac{1}{R, (R-r)}$. The \emph{intrinsic} curvature thus differs between outer and inner regions, even though the torus is embedded in flat 3D space (its \emph{extrinsic} curvature is encoded in how it bends in space). The ratio of magnitudes of inner to outer curvature is \begin{equation} \label{eq:torus-curv-ratio} \frac{|K_{\text{inner}}|}{K_{\text{outer}}} ;=; \frac{R + r}{,R - r,},, \end{equation} a dimensionless number purely determined by geometry. Notably, if one chooses $\frac{R}{r} \approx 2.46$, this cu
theory.technical_interpretation: Geometry: Curvature of Spheres and Tori: K_inner is an algebraic definition, written K_inner = -,(1)/(R, (R-r)). It is an algebraic definition expressing the quantity directly in terms of the variables on its right-hand side. It is built from R, r, defining K_inner. Within the Trawin Zero Point registry it serves as one element of the framework's mathematical narrative.
